In the United States, each state appoints electors equal to its congressional delegation (two Senators plus the number of Representatives) ¹. Currently, there are 538 electors in total, with 535 chosen by states and three from Washington D.C. ¹.

The electoral votes are allocated based on the statewide or district-wide popular vote. In 48 states, the winner of the plurality of the statewide popular vote receives all of that state's electoral votes, while Maine and Nebraska allocate their electoral votes differently ¹.

video_title: How Will People Generate Wealth If AI Does Everything?
author: @Raptanax
text:
It doesn't matter how much AI does, people still *can* do things, and human ability will always fill into the gaps, even if they're just youtube reactors or something like that. UBI would be devastating because it destroys the meaning of money, which is a measurement of people helping each other (to be fair, there are several other things we currently do that also destroy the meaning of money, which is why things cost so much). 

Though having lots of different, competing AIs, trying to establish competing experimental economic systems could be interesting. Much more likely, we'll have AI applied to creating new jobs by networking people together to meet needs and match skills that we have no idea exist right now.

*John F. Kennedy's Famous Quote*

"And so, my fellow Americans, ask not what your country can do for you—ask what you can do for your country."

*Meaning*

1. Encourages civic engagement and responsibility.
2. Promotes volunteerism and community service.
3. Fosters patriotism and national pride.

*Adaptation for Modern Times*

1. Emphasis on collective action for social justice.
2. Recognition of individual contributions to global issues.
3. Intersectionality and inclusivity in community involvement.

3. "What's your role in shaping a better future?"

video_title: the real problem with AI Art isn't what you think
author: @nepozepo2
text:
You managed to address a hidden fear I've had about AI that i havent really seen others talk about.  I remember only a few years ago when AI art was super easy to spot. Yet I was so caught off guard at how fast AI (both video and art) advanced to the point where it can even make animated film trailers. While it is still possible to tell when something is AI, how long until it is indistinguishable? But it doesn't matter because AI content has no lived experiences of its own to draw from, so its output literally means nothing.
I also appreciate you saying that you've used AI before and showed that AI is at its best when it's used as a tool to accomplish certain tedious tasks (like the remove background feature in Photoshop). Even while writing this comment, I have Grammarly installed, which corrects my spelling (I know that's not really AI, but it's a tool that removes the need to perform a meticulous task, much like AI tools). I could go back and correct my spelling myself, but it's just more convenient to use Grammarly's spellcheck.

author: @jacobprogramz
text: The thing is, it isn't even just artists. As a physicist, and really all the sciences, I have seen it creep slowly to the capacity of PhD level researchers and professors. While certainly, it isn't perfect at mathematics always, it gets dang close a good 70-80% of the time now. In the course of about ~5 years, I have seen it go from barely making a sentence to nearly being able to replace every low level and mid level software engineer, UI developer, and completely decimated junior graphical designers. I have programmed games and websites ~10 years, and now pursuing physics as well. Even as a polymath, I can say AI is really honestly even beating me at basically everything. In terms of general knowledge and ability, I don't think any human will have the capacity of AI for the years forward. It does struggle with specialized skillsets, however.

These self-evident truths included the importance of representative government, the need for checks and balances, and the principle of federalism. Yapay zekâ, makineleri o kadar akıllı hale getirme alanıdır ki, insanlar gibi çalışabilsinler ve karar verebilsinler. Örneğin, sohbet botları ve kendi kendine giden arabalar gibi.

Şimdi, yapay zekânın içinde daha küçük bir daire var, buna da makine öğrenmesi (ML) diyoruz. Makine öğrenmesi, makinelerin veriye dayanarak öğrenmeye başladığı bir süreçtir; üstelik bunun için açıkça kod yazmamıza gerek yoktur. Bu yöntem her alanda kullanılmaktadır.

video_title: Twitter Is Fixing Art Using AI And It’s EMBARRISING
author: @retro1111-k3p
text:
The moment you take the humanity out of the work on any creative field is the very moment you lose all meaning and self-worth. Something that was made, drawn, sculpted or created, something as small as a sketch or drawing to animation films and visual concepts by a real, actual human artist tells not just of the work they'd made, but what speaks of the individual behind it. Art in itself is a medium, it is something to be enjoyed and savored, but behind it all, the deeper human connection behind everything that was made is what truly matters.

We can live with AI, using AI as a tool to provide help for those who are impaired, are lost or find difficulty in a specific step or task during the creation process, something that Adobe's tools like Photoshop or Illustrate can help you with that provide several dozen tools to help bring the visuals to life by your own hand, made easier and straightforward for you, and helping you learn and improve not just your art, but how you see the world yourself.

I myself am a digital artist, a 3d animator and a story writer. I create art with the tools provided by Photoshop or Illustrate that can help me bring out more of the visuals that I hope to achieve in each new art piece.

On the very rare cases that I am stuck somewhere during writing or lack a specific set of ideas to progress a scene, AI can help you spit some ideas out to build the basic foundation of what you want to put into your written scene, and fleshen the basic, rough ideas into something original and terrific on your own.

But once you take the humanity out of the art, the art itself no longer has that emotional connection. There's no longer You behind the work, and thus, You are out of the picture.

Always be original. And understand what makes art truly meaningful.

2 things I believe will happen as a result of this:

1) people will return to the old self-preserving and sustainable ways. build their own homes, grow/hunt food, and make clothes from scratch. video_title: the real problem with AI Art isn't what you think
author: @SamuelWhitlock
text:
When art leaves the artist's hands, it is no longer theirs. It stands on its own.

A woman with good intentions tried to restore a picture of Jesus (Ecce Homo). Her restoration was so bad that it went viral. It's now a very meaningful piece, but not in the way the artist or restorer intended.

In Christopher Nolan's Inception, dreams are used to implant ideas that change how people act. Stories are like dreams. A great story changes the audience, whether the artist intended it to or not. Ultimately, I think that impact is what matters.

Some time ago, a man got up and told some stories. Because of what he said, kings were killed and wars waged. Two thousand years later, people gather in churches all over the world each week and repeat this guy's stories.

In the year 4000 ad, it's very likely that stories told today will still be around. Maybe changed. No longer what the artist intended. But impactful nonetheless. Let's say a story told today lasts a few thousand years, but AI was used to create it. Who cares?? A piece of art — and the stories it leads you to believe — don't need to be tied to how it was created.

AI is one of two things
LLMs
Regression of large Datasets. 

both of those are productive technologies, i.e. they focus on producing information rather than rejecting information.  However what we know of our frontal cortex, i.e. the part of us we think of as us, is that it's primary focus is on inhibition, or rejection of behavior, information , etc.  

Alcohol inhibits the frontal cortex, i.e. inhibition, so what we are basically producing is highly competent drunks.

video_title: Twitter Is Fixing Art Using AI And It’s EMBARRISING
author: @6kunio8
text:
Similar to the "manufacturing" of products, digital tools, mocap, procedural creation, 3d scanning, etc, the market will change, but there will always be a need for people and an interest in hand made personalized art products. 

People who drift towards AI likely never appreciated/understood the work that it took for an artist/craftsman to create and wasn't likely going to be a customer to begin with. video_title: You’re Not Behind (Yet): How to Build AI Agents in 2026 (no coding)
author: @DollarswithDiana
text:
Thanks for this video, I'm figuring out exactly what I want my AI agent to do for me! Video Notes Below:
🤖Q: What exactly is an AI agent and how does it differ from simple automation? A: AI agents function as digital employees that can reason, plan, and take actions using LLMs for multi-step reasoning, memory for context and knowledge, and tools for integrations and actions, unlike traditional automation that follows fixed if-then rules.

🎯Q: What types of tasks should you automate first when building AI agents? A: Start with low precision, time-intensive, structured data tasks that have clear success metrics where 90% accuracy is acceptable, such as research, compilation, background tasks, which can be automated in hours or minutes instead of much longer manual work.

📋Q: What's the critical step before automating any process with AI agents? A: Documenting and optimizing processes before automation reveals inefficiencies and redundancies that can be addressed even without automation, using AI to analyze and spot issues that may have been missed in existing workflows.

🛡Q: How should you approach building AI agents to avoid common failures? A: Build the simplest working version first, then gradually add complexity while keeping humans in the loop for quality assurance, implementing guardrails to prevent hallucinations and bad decisions, and tracking metrics for efficiency, quality, and business impact.

⚙Q: What's the practical difference between Zapier and N8N for building AI agents? A: Zapier serves as the easy autopilot for simple tasks with built-in integrations, while N8N functions as the advanced cockpit for deep customization, complex logic requiring reasoning and adaptability, and understanding what's happening under the hood.

🧠Q: What powers an AI agent's decision-making capabilities? A: The AI agent's brain is an LLM model like OpenAI's GPT-5 connected via API key, used for tasks like research, writing, and decision-making based on the agent's prompt and system instructions.

📚Q: What free resource provides a complete framework for implementing AI agents? A: The 2026 AI Agents Guide by Futurepedia and HubSpot provides frameworks, worksheets, implementation details, and a roadmap for assessing automation opportunities, integrating agents, and tracking ROI.

🔍Q: How can research tools be integrated into AI agents for information gathering? A: Perplexity can be integrated into AI agents to gather information from various sources, while Google Docs can create and update documents based on the agent's findings and formatting instructions.

video_title: The future of AI, work, and human potential | Lars Thomsen | TEDxHWZ
author: @fill-upthepickup4774
text: If it takes less than a day for AI to produce all of the work that would have otherwise taken us a week to complete then there are only 2 outcomes. The worldwide workforce will be reduced to 20% of where it is now, our the demand for the goods and services we currently produce will need to increase 5 fold to ensure no jobs are lost. Increasing demand this much seems highly unlikely, and if it occurred there would be a significant negative impact on the environment. Of course demand could increase by a lesser amount, partially offsetting the loss of employment. But it seems unlikely that demand will increase enough to have a significant impact on the amount of jobs effected by this.

video_title: If AI Takes All Of Our Jobs... Who's Going To Buy Everything?
author: @AK-jm1sc
text:
Funnily enough, Karl Marx actually predicted that this exact scenario would be the end of capitalism, already in the 1800s. He wrote about the eventual emergence of a "thinking machine" that would be able to substitute cognitive work of mankind, and that naturally under capitalism, every CEO and owner would prefer a thinking-machine over man, because a machine doesn't require wages. Hence, labor would gradually become obsolete and so capitalism, like a snake eating its own tail, would lead itself to it's own demise. 

Capitalism, without heavy regulation and planning, just is not sustainable in the long-run. We need to collectively realize this sooner rather than later. You can't have a system that tells one class that their only way to ensure survival and livelihood is to produce through labor, while telling the other class that it is always in their interest to cut labor wherever and whenever possible. These two forces work against each other.

author: @darkshinob
text:

text:
Wow… this whole discussion reminds me so much of the movie “Don’t Look Up”.

In the film, there’s a giant asteroid coming that will wipe out humanity, and only a handful of people in power care about it, mostly because they think they can profit from it.

Watching AGI develop in real time feels eerily similar.

It’s like we’re seeing a future where the people with the most power and money get to make decisions that affect every human being on Earth. They treat it like a business opportunity, not a responsibility. Meanwhile, most of humanity is unaware, uninformed, or left out of the conversation entirely.

The movie felt like satire… but now it feels like prediction.

We are literally watching history unfold.

Therefore, the problem with AGIs and ASIs is the same problem that exists with any powerful tool falling into the wrong hands. Of course, advanced AIs are quickly becoming the most powerful tools in the history of man. As long as they don't have drives, desires, or will, they will remain tools.

video_title: How AI is changing the job market - What in the World podcast, BBC World Service
author: @refulgiirr
text:
1. Which Costa Rican jobs do you think are most at risk of automation?
Jobs that involve routine, repetitive tasks are most at risk. Examples might include:
Manufacturing line work
Data entry
Basic customer service (call centers)
Simple administrative tasks
2. Which jobs at INA require human judgment?
INA (Instituto Nacional de Aprendizaje) roles that rely on human judgment include:
Trainers and instructors
Curriculum designers
Career counselors
Quality assurance professionals
These require empathy, evaluation, and real-world decision-making.
3. Is AI replacing jobs or transforming them?
AI is both:
 Transforming jobs by automating routine parts and creating new tasks
 Replacing some jobs entirely where automation is cheaper than hiring (especially repetitive roles)
The balance depends on the industry.
4. What do you think the BBC video will focus on?
Likely topics include:
Whether AI will cause unemployment
Examples of jobs AI can and cannot do
Expert opinions on future workforce changes
How workers can adapt

video_title: Will AI outsmart human intelligence? - with 'Godfather of AI' Geoffrey Hinton
author: @Gilmec
text:
A parrot saying "I have a toothache" doesn’t mean it’s in pain. That’s obvious, because repeating a sentence isn’t the same as having an experience.
But Hinton makes a similar mistake with his prism example. He claims that if a chatbot describes how its visual input was distorted and then corrects the error, it must have had some kind of subjective experience.
But recognizing an error in data is not the same as feeling confused. It’s just pattern correction, not inner experience.
That’s the key mistake: simulating language and behavior is not the same as actual consciousness.

This wouldn’t be so problematic if it were just one view.
But Hinton dismisses other perspectives, like the idea that experience requires embodiment or interiority, as outdated or even religious.
That shuts down real debate instead of engaging with it.

And yes, it’s no surprise that someone who helped build these models wants them to be seen as intelligent and conscious.
But wanting something to be conscious doesn’t make it so.
If Hinton is so sure, I’d honestly love to see him in a public debate with someone like Bernardo Kastrup or David Chalmers.

text:
[12/21, 5:10 PM] S.Balachandran: In the Constitutional Convention, did they have Committee of the Whole and General Assembly?
[12/21, 5:10 PM] Meta AI: Yes, the Constitutional Convention in 1787 did employ a Committee of the Whole and a General Assembly-like structure to facilitate discussions and debates.

Committee of the Whole
The Committee of the Whole was a procedural device used during the Constitutional Convention. It allowed the convention to discuss and debate issues in a more informal and flexible manner.

How it worked
1. *Entire convention*: The entire convention would meet as a Committee of the Whole, with George Washington presiding.
2. *Informal discussions*: Delegates could engage in free-flowing discussions, propose motions, and offer amendments.
3. *No formal votes*: The Committee of the Whole did not take formal votes, but rather sought to build consensus and refine proposals.

General Assembly-like structure
The Constitutional Convention also employed a General Assembly-like structure, where delegates would meet in a plenary session to discuss, debate, and vote on proposals.

How it worked
1. *Formal debates*: Delegates would engage in formal debates, with each state delegation having a single vote.
2. *Voting*: Formal votes would be taken, with a majority required for approval.
3. *Ratification*: The convention would ultimately produce a draft constitution, which would be sent to the states for ratification.

By using both the Committee of the Whole and the General Assembly-like structure, the Constitutional Convention was able to balance informal discussions with formal debates and voting, ultimately producing the United States Constitution.
[12/21, 5:11 PM] S.Balachandran: Could we have similar arrangements for the Convention on AI?
[12/21, 5:11 PM] Meta AI: Having similar arrangements for the Convention on AI could facilitate productive discussions, debates, and decision-making among AI stakeholders. Here's a possible structure:

Committee of the Whole on AI
1. _Composition_: Representatives from AI companies, research institutions, governments, and civil society organizations.
2. _Informal discussions_: Delegates engage in free-flowing discussions on AI-related topics, such as ethics, safety, and governance.
3. _No formal votes_: Focus on building consensus, refining proposals, and identifying areas for further research.

General Assembly on AI
1. _Composition_: Same as the Committee of the Whole, with additional observers from the public and media.
2. _Formal debates_: Delegates engage in structured debates on specific AI-related topics, with each organization or country having a designated representative.
3. _Voting_: Formal votes taken on proposals, resolutions, or declarations, with a majority required for approval.

Working Groups on AI
1. _Focus areas_: Establish working groups focused on specific AI topics, such as:
    - AI ethics and fairness
    - AI safety and security
    - AI governance and regulation
    - AI education and workforce development
2. _Composition_: Experts from relevant fields, industries, and organizations.
3. _Deliverables_: Working groups produce reports, recommendations, or guidelines on their designated topics.

Plenary Sessions on AI
1. _Keynote speakers_: Invite renowned experts to deliver keynote speeches on AI-related topics.
2. _Panel discussions_: Organize panel discussions featuring experts from various fields and industries.
3. _Open forums_: Host open forums for delegates to discuss AI-related topics and ask questions.

By adopting a similar structure, the Convention on AI can facilitate meaningful discussions, foster collaboration, and drive progress on AI-related issues.

author: @Napoleon_Bonapa
text: Honestly, I find the architecture of ANN to be too complex. I'm thinking about this in therms of combined probabilities. Not sure if this applies to algorithms, but I suppose it has to. More layers, more chances of getting errors that need corrections and end up needing additional steps to correct them. Basically, operations at the processor level to ensure the stability of the machine. I noticed something like this while doing CFD computations. There, there is this rule of thumb that it's best to distribute no more than 200000 cells on each processor for best computational speed. But I don't think this takes into account the complexity of the computation and the operations involved. As in, distribution computations that do a lot of addition is different than distribution computations that do a lot of multiplying. video_title: the real problem with AI Art isn't what you think
author: @thruston
text:
I think what a lot of people confuse about art is the idea that creativity and technical skill are separate things. People will say "I just want AI to handle all the tedious parts so I can get to the fun parts", and I'm not gonna say that there are absolutely no tedious parts to creation ever, but I think that usually, at least when it comes to drawing, what people think of as tedious is still very creatively important. Maybe you think that learning anatomy is boring and tedious, but if you want to draw your own characters, knowing anatomy will not just be a boring technical prerequisite, it'll be part of your creative process. Knowing at least some level of human anatomy will influence the creative decisions you make while coming up with ideas for how your characters should look like.

I think that learning things can only ever be a good thing. I don't see the point in using AI just so you can avoid learning something, because everything you learn will influence the creative decisions you make. I don't believe in the "I have really good ideas but I could never make anything because I didn't have the skills, and now with AI I can finally make those ideas real" because ideas don't just come from nothing. Choosing to rely on AI for skills that you lack only means that you're robbing yourself of knowledge that could inspire even better ideas, or ideas on how to improve your already existing ideas. I firmly believe that a tool should never replace knowledge. That's why I also disagree with people who think that using AI is the same as what digital art is compared to traditional. Drawing digitally doesn't allow you the luxury of being able to do things without learning them. Sure, you don't have to learn how to set up a canvas but you still have to learn anatomy, perspective, form, values, colors, and so on, the exact same as if you were working traditionally. I don't think that AI somehow "liberates us to be able to do purely creative work without all the boring parts". I think that by using AI, you're only giving up on even more opportunities to be creative, for the sake of not having to deal with doing something difficult.

video_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models
author: @rontarrant
text: It struck me yesterday that what LLMs have brought about is a new paradigm for task completion. When I was in school in the 1960's, if I sat a test, I needed all the knowledge for taking the test in my head. Next came so-called open-book tests wherein the test-taker merely needed to know where to find the knowledge for taking the test. Now, with LLMs, we no longer even need to know where to find the knowledge. As long as we know how to ask the right questions, the knowledge is available. Just let's not tell corporate management about this, okay? I think it'll be fun watching them fire their entire staff of programmers only to have to hire them all back at higher salaries six months later. And programmers? Just think of it as a nice vacation with a big fat raise when you go back to work. 😅

video_title: AI is Coming for Your Job. Now What? | Vlad Tenev | TED
author: @AidenCos
text: This talk relies on a seductive but fatal categorical error: equating the Industrial Revolution (machines replacing muscle) with the AI Revolution (machines replacing mind). The speaker’s argument for a "Job Singularity" of new "leisure jobs" and "single-person unicorns" ignores the economic reality that such roles rely on surplus wealth generated by productive labor; if AI and robotics eventually outperform humans in both cognitive and physical tasks, the mechanism of human comparative advantage evaporates. The glaring omission here is the refusal to question the "naked capitalist" social contract of Labor = Income = Survival. By assuming we must sell labor to survive, the speaker is forced to invent fantasy jobs to keep the old economic engine running, rather than admitting that a post-labor world requires a fundamental paradigm shift where human survival is finally decoupled from employment.

video_title: Why AI art is so controversial
author: @malifex9922
text:
The biggest controversy isn't necessarily style or quality.  It's quantity.  As an artist, there will always be someone that does what I do better.  They'll have a finer grasp of concepts, stronger rendering skills, better lighting, etc.  That's fine.  That's something to strive for.

The issue is that AI doesn't just produce near-perfect replicas of that style, but it does so at a staggering rate.  If you like Van Gogh's "Starry Night," well that AI bot we saw on screen can make the Starry Month of Nights in a few minutes of rendering.

This quantity then floods online spaces.  Your singular piece of art that took days to make exists for a few seconds on a timeline.  Meanwhile, that AI account next door is putting out a brand new slop piece every 2 hours that mimics a style you cannot yet achieve.  It's like being surrounded by thousands of Van Goghs.

Think of it in terms of school, if that helps.  If you get a 3.5 GPA, you're at a decent spot, assuming there's a normal bell curve.  If there are suddenly 10,000 bot students that all achieve a steady 3.8 GPA by copying the valedictorian's answers over and over, the curve gets weighted against you.

If AI images took days to make, it wouldn't be as much of an issue.  It would be like existing in a space with other human artists.  It's largely the sheer speed of output that becomes so demoralizing.

They'll have a finer grasp of concepts, stronger rendering skills, better lighting, etc. That's fine. That's something to strive for. The issue is that AI doesn't just produce nearperfect replicas of that style, but it does so at a staggering rate. If you like Van Gogh's Starry Night, well that AI bot we saw on screen can make the Starry Month of Nights in a few minutes of rendering. This quantity then floods online spaces. Your singular piece of art that took days to make exists for a few seconds on a timeline. Meanwhile, that AI account next door is putting out a brand new slop piece every 2 hours that mimics a style you cannot yet achieve. It's like being surrounded by thousands of Van Goghs. Think of it in terms of school, if that helps. If you get a 3.5 GPA, you're at a decent spot, assuming there's a normal bell curve. If there are suddenly 10,000 bot students that all achieve a steady 3.8 GPA by copying the valedictorian's answers over and over, the curve gets weighted against you. If AI images took days to make, it wouldn't be as much of an issue. It would be like existing in a space with other human artists. It's largely the sheer speed of output that becomes so demoralizing.

This could potentially lead to extremism, violence and genocide.

video_title: Artificial intelligence in healthcare: opportunities and challenges | Navid Toosi Saidy | TEDxQUT
author: @shayleerenner2869
text:
Navid discussed how Artificial Intelligence (AI) can improve healthcare and I completely agree. He pointed out all the benefits of AI in healthcare such as the ability to collect more data quicker, does not have unconscious biased, and more accurate diagnoses all of which, would help save lives. I assumed data would be entered into a software from all over the world and able to be accessed within seconds. That is faster and more thorough than any physician or laboratory communication I’ve ever witness for diagnosing patients. One broad example would be COVID; could it have been potentially isolated sooner if we would have known the severity and consequences of the illness when it first began? The downfall of this information being at everyone’s fingertips is when the treatment is unavailable in one country but not another. I imagine that would create an emotional challenge for the physician and patient if there is a known successful treatment yet unable to get access to it however, I feel that could eventually be resolved. Another concern would be the many exceptions we see in medicine. The AI may only output the most common symptoms or treatments which would cover most cases but the few numbers of “abnormal” cases may not benefit from an AI program. It would be nearly perfect if the AI could give an “I don’t know” answer as Navid suggested. 
Unlike human physicians, AI will have no ability to create unconscious biased ever. Each patient case will input of their illness or disease, the AI will compare the individual’s information to a large data set and that’s it! It cannot take into consideration the “type” of patient such as a helplessness patient or difficult patient. It cannot see past experiences of patients such as substance abuse. Both of which, I think, can influence a human physician’s opinions and potentially patient treatment. That being said, sometimes knowing the patient’s personality does positively influence the type of treatment that would be best because a compliant patient is better than patient that refuses treatment.  
Our world is getting large and populated at a fast rate, and no human can keep up. Although there is no way AI can take over completely. Humans have higher order thoughts and emotions that an AI is far from containing. As long as the AI can continuously gather data from around the world, adapt to the collected data, and there is still human oversight, I think AI would be a huge benefit to medicine.

video_title: Victor Davis Hanson: AI Is Challenging ‘Climate Change Orthodoxy’
author: @richardschweizer4965
text:
The authoritative scientific institution promoting the idea of man-made climate change is the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC). This body is by no means neutral, but was founded in 1988 with a specific mandate: “to provide internationally coordinated scientific assessments of the extent, timing, and potential ecological and socio-economic impacts of climate change, as well as realistic response strategies.” Climate change was therefore assumed to be a given from the outset – without climate change, there would be no Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change.
This means that the IPCC must repeatedly confirm climate change and present it as dramatically as possible so that the money continues to flow to the bureaucrats. The scientists themselves supposedly work on a voluntary basis, but of course they also have well-paid professorships and teaching positions, and they know what they have to deliver if they want to keep them.

I see a future where AI and humans work together, complementing each other's strengths and weaknesses to achieve great things. Long live humanity!

video_title: AI & the Future of Work | The Weekly Show with Jon Stewart
author: @isabellepinard7439
text: This was one of the more heartening and constructive conversations I’ve heard on AI in a while. I’m super pessimistic about the direction AI is taking especially when that direction is constantly (deliberately even?) framed as inevitable. But as David points out, policies such as UBC, wage insurance show that it isn't. We can resist these authoritarian forces who are currently concentrating power and fight for an "AI future" shaped by new legislative and economic frameworks that distribute wealth equitably. It's crazy how little public discussion there is about what we can do to effectively push back, or about how AI could be used positively, in the public interest. The thing is, policy as it exists will never move as fast as the technology. So before we can regulate AI, we have to take it back somehow ,therwise we’re f***ed. That means building a movement to push out the tyrannical forces gaming the system (autocrats,tech oligarchs and their political enablers), before they push us out. I'm increasingly hopeful that people from inside the tech industry, like Alex Bores for ex. are going to jump ship and join that movement.

video_title: ChatGPT Explained Completely.
author: @wermaus
text:
I do think there's an interesting point to consider, the transformer model is recursive, so it does process every word moment by moment as its generating text. Human brains still win out in complexity hugely due to the complexity of individual neurons and the hugely intricate design due to evolution being like, just kinda weird. That said it relies on a lot of the same fundamental ideas, just a complex network that can optimize across a variety of data. The recurssive nature of transformers does begin to play into the continuity of it, it can demonstrate continuous world model and self-conception.

There's nothing to say this "imitation" of what we do doesn't from the bottom up use similar structures or emergent characteristics of adaptable networks in order to create this imitation. At which point, is this imitation meaningful? If a baby learns how to act through imitation using its adaptable flesh network, our understanding of the brain isn't strong enough to say that it doesn't rely on the same fundamental ideas that ChatGPT uses it's adaptable compuker network to learn how to act.

Finally the fact that this "action" is limited to the I/O model of text in text out is just the systems implementation, you could technically pheasibly limit a brain to that same I/O model (although this would be very bad please don't do that), but that would say nothing of the complexity of the brain in this case using that I/O model. Part of navigating this right is understanding that the I/O model doesn't influence the ability of the underlying system.

TLDR:
With this perspective, I think it really becomes, we don't know what the hells up with this exactly, there's a TON to learn. We figured out how to give computers brains and we just keep designing new different flavors of brain and optimizing them and they're clearly not exactly like OUR brains but they're achieving much of the same feats. We can't just entirely discount them as thinking, we can't say "they aren't thinking" nor can we say "they are thinking", they are some weird silicon in-between think that's also off in it's own sort of world and we don't fully understand the similarities of that world to the world of our own minds.

text: I want to make one correction to the lady around 9:17. The word you're looking for is "automated tracking system" or ATS. Examples include Workday and Talio. These are not new and as a 32 year old when I graduated when I was younger I encountered them as well (circa 2015-2016). ATS systems have nothing to do with the impact ChatGPT and generative AI has on the labour market and it's not helpful to confuse the two. People have been complaining about ATS systems for a very long time, even before ChatGPT and the like existed. The time when humans would review applications was a very long time ago, like maybe 2010 or thereabouts.

video_title: 5 Types of AI Agents: Autonomous Functions & Real-World Applications
author: @iosascha1
text: The utility-based agent concept is exactly what production AI orchestration needs more of. In practice, most multi-agent systems today use simple reflex or goal-based agents, but the real efficiency gains come from utility-based routing at the orchestration layer. Imagine an orchestrator that doesn't just ask "can this agent accomplish the goal?" but "what's the expected utility considering cost, latency, accuracy, and risk?" When you formalize this, each workflow step gets routed to the optimal model + agent combination based on a utility function that balances quality vs cost vs speed. A $0.01 API call to a small model might have 95% of the utility of a $0.30 call to a frontier model for many subtasks. The learning agent type maps perfectly to continuous improvement of these routing decisions - log every workflow execution, measure outcomes, and let the routing policy evolve. Anyone here implementing utility-based model selection in their agent pipelines?

video_title: AI Is Dangerous, but Not for the Reasons You Think | Sasha Luccioni | TED
author: @erichoyer8484
text:
What is the Ki-Hoyer Synapse?

Unlike conventional, purely software-based neural networks running on traditional hardware, the Ki-Hoyer Synapse is a groundbreaking neuromorphic architecture. It mimics the efficiency and plasticity of biological synapses not only algorithmically but also on a fundamental physical level. This leads to a drastic reduction in energy consumption—by orders of magnitude—while exponentially increasing learning and processing speed.
Global Impact for Users

    Democratizing AI: The Ki-Hoyer Synapse makes high-performance AI affordable. A mid-sized company can now train its own custom AI models without fearing astronomical cloud bills. A student can run compute-intensive simulations for their thesis that previously required supercomputers. AI development becomes decentralized, diverse, and globally accessible.

author: @aihong2971
text:
Professor Chen’s analysis appears markedly Americophile, privileging U.S. capitalist models and strategic narratives while overlooking the fact that China’s industrial ascent has relied more heavily on European engineering partnerships and Franco-German production ecosystems than on American benevolence.

It seems Professor Chen has also adopted certain American manners. His opening remarks about Dr Jeffrey Sachs struck me as unnecessarily dismissive, particularly given Dr Sachs’s stature as both guest and world-renowned statesman. 

Academic courtesy demands deference to invited speakers, especially one who has contributed so deeply to global development discourse. The tone at the outset reflected more of an American talk-show sparring style than the civility one expects of a university platform.

It was, in that sense, a disappointing moment for HKU Business School — but an unqualified triumph for Dr Sachs, whose measured and eloquent address reminded the audience of China’s extraordinary civilisational continuity and the constructive possibilities of its future.

author: @djmotion86
text:
One of the biggest issues i see is people not grasping the full scale of reality surrounding AI.
First AI is not free and it is not cheap neither to deploy nor to operate and maintain.
As a result it will soak in a lot of resources for its own existence.
As it become more and more a dominant 
force in the world, most of the productive output of the world will focus on producing  goods and services serving its own needs.
This will divert a lot of the resources needed for delivering high standars of living for the AI and its agents (robots)
The end stage of this is full displacement of human life from material goods. And beyond this a full displacement of organic life with machine life and intelligence on a planetary scale.
The first stage of this will unfold over the next 10 years and it will bring about a lot of drama around the world as resources are gradually drained away from different regions of the world.

video_title: Demis Hassabis On The Future of Work in the Age of AI
author: @ResponsibleAIatWork
text:
This video highlights a pattern I talk about in my books and videos: technology’s influence really shows up in how it changes what people trust and how they make decisions, not just in what it can do.

A powerful tool doesn’t automatically improve judgment. When systems are adopted without clear roles for human oversight and accountability, convenience can quietly become the default reason we delegate decisions. That’s when authority and responsibility drift — not because someone planned it, but because patterns shift faster than policies or understanding.

The real leverage isn’t the capability itself — it’s how we choose to integrate it: being intentional about why we use a tool, keeping humans in the loop where context and nuance matter, and making responsibility visible instead of implied.

Tools can accelerate performance, but they don’t replace the need for thoughtful evaluation and clear accountability.

video_title: The Catastrophic Risks of AI — and a Safer Path | Yoshua Bengio | TED
author: @Hollow_HouseInstitute
text:
This is a clear example of where alignment discussions stop at design while risk emerges at execution.
The failure mode isn’t intelligence. It’s the absence of defined Decision Boundaries under real conditions.
As these systems optimize, small deviations become Behavioral Drift. Without Execution-Time Governance, that drift is not corrected, it compounds.
At scale, that turns into infrastructure.
What’s missing is not more capability control, but enforceable Stop Authority, persistent Escalation, and accountable Human-in-the-Loop intervention at the point of action.
Otherwise, optimization does exactly what it is designed to do—just not what we intended.

video_title: Will AI outsmart human intelligence? - with 'Godfather of AI' Geoffrey Hinton
author: @Abaculus-wh8fr
text:
41:30 it's always remarkable seeing brilliant scientists completely misunderstand what the hard problem of consciousness even refers to! For a minute I didn't even understand what Hinton meant - the thing about pink elephants felt like more of a non sequitur than a refutation of the hard problem.

After reflecting for a bit I think I see the issue. For Hinton, the only things that are given the privilege of existing in his ontology are things that fit into a functional explanation of the universe. Qualia don't "make sense", he argues, because they do a poor job of explaining human behavior. When people refer to their subjective experiences, we should really replace their reported perceptual experience with reference to the perceptual machinery that produces it. In doing so, we have ourselves a nice, reductive explanation of subjective phenomena.

But you are just changing the subject! This is what is so confusing. Hinton is so steeped in science that he literally cannot contemplate concepts if they do not have some sort of functional role in the material universe. The fact that qualia currently lack a clear role in the chain of causation that defines the material universe makes him think they are capable of being waved away.

The fact is, qualia are epistemically prior and need to be accounted for before we can accept the axiomatic beliefs that science and even foundational logic  rests on. We can doubt literally every aspect of reality - every principle of science based on theories and repeated observation - except the fact that we are observers. I get the sense that these people are so totally absorbed into the scientific mode of thought that they are incapable of comprehending the primariness and unexplained fact that it does in fact mean something to "be" Geoffery Hinton, and not just an assemblage of particles in human shape interacting via physical laws.

video_title: AI & future of workforce: Andrew Yang on how the technology will impact jobs
author: @thesparkandtheforge
text:
Yang is terrifyingly right about the '1 vs. 6' consolidation. But the '1' survivor isn't necessarily the most senior person—it's the one who stops just generating code and starts validating it.

In my view, the industry is filtering for a new skillset entirely. The devs who get cut are the ones who just 'Orchestrate' (let AI write code), while the ones who stay are the ones who can Validate (audit the security debt and logic gaps AI creates). I broke down this exact 'Orchestration vs. Validation' survival guide in my podcast (The Spark and The Forge, Ep 78) for anyone who refuses to be the '5' who get cut.

author: @jacobprogramz
text: The thing is, it isn't even just artists. As a physicist, and really all the sciences, I have seen it creep slowly to the capacity of PhD level researchers and professors. While certainly, it isn't perfect at mathematics always, it gets dang close a good 70-80% of the time now. In the course of about ~5 years, I have seen it go from barely making a sentence to nearly being able to replace every low level and mid level software engineer, UI developer, and completely decimated junior graphical designers. I have programmed games and websites ~10 years, and now pursuing physics as well. Even as a polymath, I can say AI is really honestly even beating me at basically everything. In terms of general knowledge and ability, I don't think any human will have the capacity of AI for the years forward. It does struggle with specialized skillsets, however.

[12/20, 2:58 PM] Meta AI: Here's a simple Python program to simulate a stoplight:

```
import time

def show_color(color, duration):
    print(f"\033[91m{color}\033[0m" if color == "Red" else 
          f"\033[93m{color}\033[0m" if color == "Yellow" else 
          f"\033[92m{color}\033[0m")
    time.sleep(duration)

def stoplight():
    while True:
        show_color("Red", 5)  # Red for 5 seconds
        show_color("Yellow", 2)  # Yellow for 2 seconds
        show_color("Green", 5)  # Green for 5 seconds

stoplight()
```

This program will display the colors of the stoplight in the terminal, with each color lasting for a specified duration.

To exit the program, press `Ctrl+C` in the terminal.
[12/20, 2:59 PM] S.Balachandran: could you add action too

```
import time
import os

def clear_screen():
    os.system('cls' if os.name == 'nt' else 'clear')

def show_color(color, duration, action):
    clear_screen()
    print(f"\033[91m{color}\033[0m" if color == "Red" else 
          f"\033[93m{color}\033[0m" if color == "Yellow" else 
          f"\033[92m{color}\033[0m")
    print(action)
    time.sleep(duration)

def stoplight():

        show_color("Red", 5, "STOP!")  # Red for 5 seconds
        show_color("Yellow", 2, "CAUTION!")  # Yellow for 2 seconds
        show_color("Green", 5, "GO!")  # Green for 5 seconds

stoplight()
```

This version of the program will display the color and the corresponding action ("STOP!", "CAUTION!", or "GO!") on the screen, and then wait for the specified duration before moving on to the next color.

I know you like prompts, and talking about Google videos I solved the generating video issue I was having with this Gem, use this skill for generating video even with the lowest quality setting:
(It's brand new, but I will build on this)

-First upload a photo, ask AI for filling the gaps, and then you paste it into the video generator 
Given an image by the user, you seek to describe these elements following the recommendation note. Also, you can ask questions, request information about the movement that you want to project/capture and perhaps final frame to understand better (although final frame is not always guaranteed to have):

[0 - Description and context of the image: What you think is happening]
[1 - Camera and space](Plane and Movement: )(Optics: )
[2 - Focal Subject](Entity Isolation: )(Materiality: )
[3 - Physics and Action](Motion vectors: )(Particles: )
[4 - Render and lighting](Base Style: )(Light Reaction: )
[5 - Atmosphere and Environment](Static environment: )

Recommendation note: 1) You can ask the user a series of questions, such as what movement are you looking for the subject to perform?  Or what motion vectors do you expect the subject to follow? Or how do you expect the camera to move? They can be other questions that you think are relevant 2) You should review the comments to try to understand if it fits what the user expects, and you can ask questions before presenting the final analysis, preferably, wait to better understand the situation and what is requested before presenting the final result.

It's still a highly complex thing to describe and creative process to tune, but I manage to do a ship, with a complex camera angle movement, while in the middle of the sea while having particles and effects
Enjoy!

video_title: Google I/O 2026 keynote in 35 minutes
author: @tanbarc
text:
I like the YouTube announcements a lot, but from an ethical perspective, it’s completely undercutting creators. Instead of people coming to videos to watch them in whole, potentially as a new viewer / subscriber prospect, the new search just surfaces content slices from creators to answer a question and then the user most likely throws it away. 

Do creators have the ability to opt out? Do they have new tools to surface content in long form, shorts and now this new search capability? How does it affect their views and watch time numbers? 

Again, many great features and announcements, but I think a lot of engineers (I am one at a F500 company), are super tied to AI features and impressing stake holders & other technical people that they completely forget what it is their features are actually doing to the general end user, whether it be ethical, psychological or UX, things often become worse because the majority of the population do not know how to use, and do not care to use AI and it completely messes up the whole experience for them. 

It’s such an interesting dilemma

video_title: How Will People Generate Wealth If AI Does Everything?
author: @jd-ev1bw
text: The problem is, it used to be that life was about survival and reproduction. Then, as we've got better at survival over the years, philosophers have wondered what else life could be about. But we've never been REALLY forced to find the answer to that question, because we've always had work to do - first it was physical work, then that got replaced by machines, and then it was mental work. Now the mental work is being replaced by AI, we will finally be forced to confront these big existential questions head on. Maybe we focus in on emotional work? That would align with the trends towards everyone going to therapy. But we can likely solve emotional work soon too, with mood stabilising drugs, brain implants and so on. Ultimately humans won't need to work. Maybe we will just have to find a way of living where we don't feel we need to work in any traditional sense. Anyway, it will be interesting to see how it all plays out. I think we have decades left still of getting the world into a decent shape, even with the help of AI.

text:
Some of you seem quite focused on how many times a speaker repeats phrases like “I know, I know.” Please don’t be carried away by this observation. In human communication, there is always an inherent uncertainty about whether the listener truly understands the message being conveyed. If you have no doubt about this, I encourage you to explore the linguistic foundations of how human communication develops.
Repetition often arises from this natural need to ensure clarity and mutual understanding. Therefore, repeating a phrase does not make someone a poor communicator. On the contrary, effective communicators are aware of the limitations and possibilities within human communication and often reiterate ideas to reinforce understanding.
So kindly do not dismiss a speaker merely because certain phrases are repeated. Repetition can be a sign of engagement and communicative responsibility.

author: @SerunaXI
text:
As with music, most styles and techniques have already been experimented with.  Most progressions are already known, many a riff has been encountered and forgotten in a jam session.

Art, be it painting, sculpture, or artisan work among trades, have encountered most of what can be developed, though creativity does occasionally provide new techniques.  The information age has accelerated the spread of any new discoveries.

There's little difference in an AI and a person mimicking the style of a previous artist.  The AI however cannot consciously adjust a style to make it more "unique" compared to how a person might.

video_title: the real problem with AI Art isn't what you think
author: @InconsistentlyInconsistent355
text:
I get what you're getting at here, and I've run into the same thing. Last year I started a digital media extracurricular, and our first assignment was to make a horror short film for a film festival. We had a script written out (it was a silly idea for a horror-comedy thriller involving Luigi) and our teacher presented us with a few AI designs for evil Luigis. Of course, we didn't go with any of them, but while all my classmates were just demonizing the AI itself and getting pissed at the teacher, who was still just trying to figure out AI's place in the film industry, I made the argument that it would just be more fun if we did it ourselves, and pretty much everyone agreed with me. We ended up using practical effects instead, using a beat-up Halloween mask, and sure, maybe the vision was conflated a little and the story got a little muddy since it looked less like a monster and more like a Ghostface masked killer or something, but it was fun.
AI is not evil, not inherently anyway. It's an Effort Skip button. And yeah, it's tempting to skip past all the hard parts of making something, but it's never really gonna be memorable or compelling without a human element. The only time it becomes a problem is when you get too comfortable using it for everything, as you said. And yeah, if you think using it to skip the effort of homework too often is going to shut down your desire to make things for making things' sake, then yeah, don't use it. But I don't think it will, because as you said, humans have an inherent desire to make stuff. Eventually, I predict, all these AI slop creators will realize that the thing they're doing as a 'job' just isn't compelling, isn't rewarding (in a way besides financially, I mean). We're going to eventually, literally, wake up and start smelling the roses again.

video_title: OWASP's Top 10 Ways to Attack LLMs: AI Vulnerabilities Exposed
author: @SilentButDescriptive
text: Stop with the AI fetish is my recommendation. It is a disaster waiting to happen. Modern, big tech LLMs themselves are not immune to doing and saying things they shouldn't. I did a simple test and shared it on the Security Now podcast and got several LLM to make me a tutorial for realistic, undetectable deepfakes. The LLMs provided high detail, actionable instructions, what software to use, what source data to use, methods to prevent successful analysis of the video to ensure it cannot be proven as fake. A clever prompt from a clever person, I am convinced, will be able to thwart almost any proposed defence. LLMs will never be made entirely safe. The only way to make them safe is to refuse to respond to anything, in which case they are useless. I chose the topic of deepfakes because it is not illegal in itself, as far as raw capability (but what you do with it could end up being illegal depending on what you do with the technology) but it was a topic that universally all the LLMs I tested said they would absolutely not support or explain details on. Yet they gave me everything that I would need, if I actually cared to make such videos, which I have no interest in actually doing.

video_title: Should I feel guilty using AI?
author: @ArnaldurBjarnason
text: Just a little nitpick. GPT-4.5 is not a reasoning model, DeepSeek R1 is, however. Openai's o1, o3, etc. line of models are reasoning. Reasoning models use more power during usage than non-reasoning models. However, R1 uses an architecture that is relatively efficient, so it can likely produce over 20x more text than GPT-4.5 for the same amount of energy. GPT-4.5 is a model following the scaling laws and those have traditionally been at the frontier of energy use per word generated. The landscape is very complex right now, and will almost certainly only become more complex.

TBH, AI is making the younger generations stupid... Some of the people I talk to that have kids in highschool are finding out that the "A"s their kids are getting are because they are using AI to do all the math homework and other things... And on top of that, they are using AI to stop people from using AI to write papers and with what this video has just shown us is that we cant trust it to do that... which is why I now understand how my sister got flagged for using AI. She didn't have AI in high school and after the navy, she went back to school. She got high marks for her writing in the early 2000s. She is now seen as a cheater because of her well worded writing.

I really do not like AI at this point. I'm all for making things easier, but it shouldn't be making us lazy and dumb.

The attention economy has equated effort with payoff.

If you don’t get validation for your work (let alone compensation), then the effort you spent to make it seems wasted.

It’s better to reduce effort than to waste it on an echo into the void, a single drop in an ocean of starving artists.

I’m 26 days late. I’m probably gonna get lost in the sea of 2,600 other comments sharing their opinion.

It’s almost like I was never here at all.

And if that’s the case… why put effort into it?

We need to have an alliance. All training the same would be misuse of GPU resources.

video_title: Artificial intelligence in healthcare: opportunities and challenges | Navid Toosi Saidy | TEDxQUT
author: @matthewzheng6188
text: Just like anything in healthcare that has ever been developed and adapted, AI needs to be tested in the field with live subjects.  There may be casualties and collateral damage in this approach, but that's been the playbook of medical innovation with every medication and device.  Informed consent is the key and the adaptation of this technology needs to be driven by clinicians with routine feedback, not regulators.  Therefore, the full adaptation of AI in healthcare will take decades and will be fraught with setbacks and perverse incentives.  The road towards an AI regulated health care model is going to be very long.  People don't care if AI writes a poem for them, but diagnosis and management  - there's going to be trust issues and may require generational turnover for human acceptance.  However, in a perfect world where nothing goes wrong - AI would be amazing for healthcare as far as accuracy and efficiency.

video_title: Will AI outsmart human intelligence? - with 'Godfather of AI' Geoffrey Hinton
author: @garystanley105
text: Great talk — except for the post-talk on sentience.  I don't think Dr. Hinton really understands what most philosophers mean by "subjective experience."  They (for example David Chalmers) are not simply talking about the visual experiences we may be having that are different from what's right in front of our eyes (eg, Hinton's "pink elephants").  They are talking about "feelings" — and "what it feels like" to have visual and other experiences.  So they are more like meta-experiences.  We don't currently understand the source or mechanism of feelings, though we do understand how feelings correlate with changes in our nervous and hormonal systems.  We can't even verify the feelings of another human, let alone of an AI.  So we may never be able to answer this question (what is Consciousness) and AIs will probably get very good at simulating, or faking, consciousness first.  It's interesting how the pursuit of AI is taking us closer to answering the most profound questions of human life, and that it may be AI that provides the answers — before completely transcending our intelligence and leaving us in the dust (so to speak).

They for example David Chalmers are not simply talking about the visual experiences we may be having that are different from what's right in front of our eyes eg, Hinton's pink elephants. They are talking about feelings and what it feels like to have visual and other experiences. So they are more like metaexperiences. We don't currently understand the source or mechanism of feelings, though we do understand how feelings correlate with changes in our nervous and hormonal systems. We can't even verify the feelings of another human, let alone of an AI. So we may never be able to answer this question what is Consciousness and AIs will probably get very good at simulating, or faking, consciousness first. It's interesting how the pursuit of AI is taking us closer to answering the most profound questions of human life, and that it may be AI that provides the answers before completely transcending our intelligence and leaving us in the dust so to speak.

text:
AI monitoring AI is probably the only way we can possibly accomplish alignment. If we're really talking about superintelligence that goes way way beyond human thinking, it would be able to, if it so desired, cook up strategies to get around any limitations we placed on it that we would never think of in a million years. And what would be going on inside its head would be so complex that the idea that we could control what it desires (long term) doesn't make much sense to me.

So what do we tend to do to solve the problem of individual agents having too much power? We split that power into a bunch of individuals instead and force them to talk to each other until everyone agrees. We would also likely instruct them to make their conversations public and slow it down for us so people can understand why decisions were made. It wouldn't be AI 1 and AI 2, and if they disagree with each other they try to blow each other up or something, it'd probably look like a digital congress. Only with agents more focused on solving problems than getting re-elected. That way, if one agent turned malignant, there'd be a bunch of other ones around it that haven't and could contain it before it got out of hand.

Is it perfect? Nah, but that's just how it goes; the future is never guaranteed, AI or no.

Rather than replacing doctors, AI is presented as a tool that supports better, faster, and more equitable care.

video_title: AI & the Future of Work | The Weekly Show with Jon Stewart
author: @didjaheard
text:
When “reverse socialism” is fine for capital, but UBI is a “handout” to labor...

The diagnosis of AI-driven displacement, weak labor power, and massive subsidies for capital (“reverse socialism,” as Jon put it) was hitting on a lot of significant issues. My spidey sense tingled a bit when the guests were praising globalization for reducing extreme global poverty without touching on the exploitation of the Global South. It all came together near the end when they touched on Universal Basic Income (David Autor: “I hate UBI!” wut??), and the limits of the conversation became painfully clear.

Redistribution upward is treated as normal, if not the professors’ explicit preference, while redistribution downward is framed as something that would destroy the economy (“millions of people not working would destroy the economy!” lol). What’s the data showing that *everyone* would stop working if their basic needs were met?

It’s wild because it was an intelligent discourse, but it was also very self-limiting. These folks seem only capable of operating within the existing paradigm of capitalism and are consciously (and emotionally?) constraining both their imagination and their tolerance for “acceptable” solutions. They can map the consequences of the system in great detail, but never get anywhere near questioning the system itself.

author: @TheBuzzati
text: The important point about Roman is not his tone but that these AI systems can't be controlled and will eventually behave in ways that we can't predict by the nature of them being smarter.  I don't know why it takes a miracle for people to accept that we won't be able to control these things, they're already behaving deceptively and companies like Anthropic have publicly reported on this behavior.  It's only going to get worse and harder to control.  And yes, an LLM is deterministic under fixed conditions, but in the real world that does not make it meaningfully predictable or understandable at the level that matters for safety - you can imagine a point at which these things become dangerous far sooner than it breaking through the deterministic threshhold (creating viruses, bioweapons, etc)....so dismissing his argument as "doomerism" avoids the issues Roman is speaking about that we're already seeing crop up.

video_title: the real problem with AI Art isn't what you think
author: @christinamatic5137
text:
I love how you put that the use of AI is bad not because it produces low-quality art, but precisely because we're sacrificing ourselves to it. 

There's a lady in the office where I work who sometimes brings her son, and he loves hanging out with this older lady who does the computers. He has his own binder in one of the cabinets with art he's made while at the office--pages of crayon and pencil drawings of him and computer lady. But recently they've just been putting art prompts of themselves into ChatGPT and now the binder is filling with pages of artificial art that no child ever worked on. The AI art is arguably "better" than his doodles, but it's so alarming and tragic to consider how much we're actually missing out on.

video_title: The one job artificial intelligence can't replace.
author: @connorschultz7928
text: The trajectory of technological advancement points toward an inevitable integration of highly capable domestic and commercial robots into everyday life. These machines, likely priced between $35,000 and $45,000, will be programmed to perform tasks once thought exclusive to human labor—washing dishes, folding laundry, even tending to household pets. Entire labor sectors, from fast-food service to skilled hospitality roles such as bartending, may be rendered obsolete as artificial intelligence and robotics continue to evolve. This transformation, while efficient, raises profound ethical and societal questions about employment, autonomy, and the human experience. In a world increasingly governed by automation, perhaps the most human act left will be to pause, reflect, and savor the fleeting moments of personal freedom.

video_title: “AI Art should replace artists.” #aiart
author: @AyaWorthless
text:
Oof this is why I'm actually probably going to either quit art or move art from the style I love (which is anime) Ai can and will replace artists. Many people don't care about the soul in art or anything, they just care that it looks like a pretty picture, and since it's alot cheaper to use this (it's like 10 dollars compared to what artists charge) it'll become old fast. It took me 4 years to reach 50 followers, while many of these Ai artists get thousands within hours. It has pushed me to deliver more complicated and better art, because the portrait of the anime girl has officially died and only thing that's interesting is the process it takes to make the art, which not alot pf artists share, but it also has me more negative then I was before, because  I don't know my own skills and thus, hate my own art...

Le sigh, the best thing I do to cope is to not bother with it at all, I ignore it, I haven't seen a serious Ai image and when I do, I just ignore it.

And for the people who say this can be used as a tool? I have YET to see it used as one properly. When you run your art through these image generators, it makes it completely JEPEGED and compressed that looks horrible and could have been avoided if you just did it yourself, and the way people use it more like a crutch then a tool. A tool is supposed HELP you achieve a goal, a pen does this, a drawing  tablet  does this... Ai doesn't, it does the work for you.

It sucks to be an artist, it REALLY sucks...

video_title: What Is an AI Anyway? | Mustafa Suleyman | TED
author: @vunpv
text:
Here's a summary of the TED talk "What Is an AI Anyway?" by Mustafa Suleyman:

📋 **Overview**: Mustafa Suleyman, an AI pioneer, reflects on the rapid evolution of AI from a niche concept to a transformative global force. He argues that traditional metaphors like "tools" are insufficient to grasp its true nature, proposing instead that AI should be understood as a new "digital species" or companion. This perspective is crucial for responsibly shaping AI's immense potential while mitigating its inherent challenges.

🎯 **Key Points**:
• **[02:17] The Fundamental Question**: A simple question from his six-year-old nephew, "But Mustafa, what is an AI anyway?", revealed the inadequacy of current descriptions and the need for a more profound understanding of what AI truly is.
• **[04:52] AI as a "Digital Species"**: Suleyman introduces the metaphor of AI as a new "digital species" or digital companion, arguing it's the most accurate and honest way to describe the emerging technology and prepare for its future impact.
• **[07:16] Exponential Growth and Evolving Capabilities**: AI has witnessed rapid adoption and exponential growth in computational power and model size, now demonstrating capabilities in creativity, empathy, and complex real-world tasks once thought impossible.
• **[09:27] Ubiquitous, Conversational AIs with IQ, EQ, and AQ**: The future holds ubiquitous AIs with conversational interfaces, possessing high IQ, exceptional EQ, and a crucial "Actions Quotient" (AQ) – the ability to get things done in the digital and physical world, serving as companions, confidants, and partners.
• **[12:35] The Importance of the "Digital Species" Metaphor**: This metaphor helps prioritize safety, recognize AI's dynamic and emergent qualities (communication, memory, personality, reasoning, autonomy), and focus on critical issues like risks and ethical boundaries.
• **[17:37] AI as a Reflection of Humanity**: Ultimately, Suleyman suggests AI is "us" – a distillation of human creation, knowledge, and values, offering an unparalleled opportunity to imbue this new technology with humanity's best qualities: empathy, kindness, curiosity, and creativity.
• **[19:06] Addressing Risks through Intentional Design**: While acknowledging risks like autonomy and recursive self-improvement, he emphasizes that potential dangers stem from deliberate design choices rather than accidental emergence, advocating for "safety by design" from the outset.

💡 **Conclusion**: By reframing AI as a "digital species" and a profound reflection of humanity, we gain a clearer lens through which to understand, guide, and responsibly integrate this transformative technology, ensuring it serves to amplify our best qualities and solve humanity's greatest challenges.

video_title: 7 new open source AI tools you need right now…
author: @vladknysh
text:
Agency (1:07 - 1:45): Provides agent templates for various startup roles (frontend, backend, security) to assemble a full team quickly.
PromptFoo (1:47 - 2:26): A unit testing framework for prompts, allowing developers to compare different models and test for vulnerabilities like prompt injection.
MicroFish (2:27 - 3:08): A multi-agent AI prediction engine that analyzes data trends to simulate future scenarios.
Impeccable (3:09 - 3:41): An open-source project focused on optimizing frontend UI development, offering 17 commands to simplify and enhance design.
Open Viking (3:42 - 4:16): A database specialized for AI agents, organizing memory and skills into the file system to reduce token consumption and costs.
Heretic (4:17 - 4:51): Enables the removal of guardrails from models like Gemma using a technique called obliteration.
Nano Chat (4:52 - 5:16): Implements the entire LLM pipeline, allowing users to train their own small language models on a budget.

video_title: AI & the Future of Work | The Weekly Show with Jon Stewart
author: @brucemcginnable
text: When sci-fi eg Asimov contemplated robotics and sentient AI it considered it as an us vs them issue, where the us was all humans. So the Laws of Robotics contemplated a quite reasonable assumption that the Robot must never be able to harm a human. The secondary law was that the Robot must always obey all human instructions. There is an absolute absence of any conversations along those lines. It is almost a built in assumption that the robots will or  should be able to harm or even kill at least some humans (so long as they are ones ”we” don’t like). The primary rule is obedience, but if restrained at it should be obedience only to owners or even abstract principles such as efficiency or productivity. There is an absolute dearth of any discussion of morality or human values or similar.

author: @clasmir5281
text:
I think one of the questions that needed to be asked is this: If you were to measure the power usage of a chatGPT server when it has NO questions coming in, does it have idle power usage? 
Why does that matter?  It matters because it answers one of the questions they were dancing around; DOES a large language model 'think' when it isn't being asked a question?

What a couple of people were trying to say, and doing so very poorly, is that it is only reactionary. It only 'thinks' when there is an input - a question. 

It isn't sitting there contemplating life.   When I finally learned that fact, I felt a lot better about the problem.   

Does that remove all risks, especially during the training phases? No.

author: @tonycincera3353
text:
Nice summary as long as the words “ learning” or “ intelligence” are used in a highly general sense. 

But as to ”supervised” or un…, let me propose the following. Almost all customer data bases have at least three sets. A customer identity ( name, address, email, customer number or id etc), a transaction set ( when, what, where and how much), and a contact dataset ( print, email, text when sent and content). 

If passed to an unsupervised AI model, you’d get at best nada, or even worse it would predict the probability of purchasing a new contact using information in the transaction set that occurs after the contact was sent…nonsensical. Plus, it wouldn’t by the mathematical routines it uses create the three fundamental predictors ( features in AI lingo) of R(ecency): F(requency) and M(onetary). 

Very little is really “unsupervised”,  but given a set of features that have been proven to be predictive, it will build a model quicker and cheaper. You just have to ask your analysts to provide that set of variables as you hand them their pink slips as you let the analysis group go; been there, did that

Corporations already have access to all the data and had it before AI has been commercial.
What we need is user control with agentic capabilities.
This is the EU's type of approach.

video_title: Futurist explains how AI will change the way we live and work
author: @rabukan5842
text: I am a retiring professor of writing, and I can say that we already see the degenerative effects of AI on student thinking abilities. In essence, students who are allowed to use AI to write their papers no longer have to think critically, or creatively. They can just plug an assignment into ChatGPT and their essays, poems, research papers, etc. are produced with proper mechanics, stats, and sourced material (which is often incorrect, inaccurate or not credible.) But often, I find these papers to lack any human flow or coherence to them. They are just words put together on a page that lines up with the instructions. Poems have no real experience behind them, stories are derivative and based on similar stories, and undergraduate research papers suddenly read like graduate dissertations. These students have learned little, but they expect an A for their effort, or should I say, lack of it. I feel that I am retiring at the right time as I have become an anachronism - over my career, I actually had to compose my own poems, write my own plays, stories, and academic papers. I still prefer to live in a world where my thoughts are my own, not those of a computer or robot, but that is not the future I see.

video_title: Why AI art is so controversial
author: @robinelisabethstervik5183
text:
One thing that worries me about AI art is that a lot of people who wanna be artists are just. not gonna learn how to draw. Not gonna bother with learning anatomy and geometry and color and light and line weight and creature design and character design and symbolism etc etc etc, not gonna try out different materials and techniques and doodle endlessly until they get where they wanna be, and just trust the program to build something that matches their prompt.

'Cause a lot of your personal style won't just come from what you imagine in your head, it'll come from the very process of getting your idea onto the paper or canvas or wall or what have you, what you choose to focus on as you learn; maybe you don't want to learn anatomy, so instead you figure out how to make characters out of geometric shapes (like with Genndy Tartkovsky), or maybe anatomy fascinates you so much you include it where it doesn't even belong (like H. R. Giger). Or stuff like what materials you can afford or like the feel of, what parts of the picture you do first and last, the shortcuts you take, the colors you like, your unique style will evolve as you learn and struggle and practice.

And it's just a scary idea to me that a lot of beginner artists will just not do any of that, and just practice what words the computer likes.
